Were You Asked This?
Design ChatGPT

Design a conversational AI system similar to ChatGPT that allows users to have natural language conversations and receive intelligent responses. Focus on a simple version that can handle basic chat functionality for a limited number of users.

Asked at:

OpenAI

Microsoft

Q

Qualified Health AI

Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

ChatGPT is a conversational assistant: a user types a prompt, the system runs it through a large language model with the prior turns of the chat as context, and tokens stream back as they are generated. The data model is small (users own chats, chats own an ordered list of messages) and the read path is the easy part. What makes it hard is the backend behind the stream. Plan for 100M DAU, roughly 10K new messages per second at peak, and around 60K generations in flight at any moment, where a single response can take 30 seconds on a GPU that is the most expensive thing in the system.

The interesting questions all sit between the browser and the GPU. Tokens come from an inference worker that is a separate service from the stateless web tier, so something has to carry them from the worker generating to the web pod holding that user's connection, with time to first token under about half a second. GPUs are scarce and load is bursty, so requests have to be queued, batched, and shed under pressure rather than dispatched one per worker. The pool is multi-tenant: one user's 100K-token prompt costs as much GPU as a thousand short ones, and paid tiers need to stay fast while free tiers take the hit when capacity runs out. And conversation context grows every turn, so replaying full history makes each turn slower and pricier until it overruns the context window. Interviewers use ChatGPT to probe streaming transports, GPU scheduling under load, cost-aware multi-tenancy, and context management, not whether you can wrap a model API in a CRUD app.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users can send a prompt in a chat and receive an AI-generated response.

Users can view their past chats and resume a conversation with the chat's prior context carried into the prompt.

Users can watch a response stream in token by token and cancel a generation that is still running.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you get tokens from the inference worker generating them to the stateless web pod holding the user's connection, with time to first token under ~500ms?

How do you route and schedule generation requests across a constrained pool of GPU inference workers as load grows?

How do you stop heavy users from monopolizing the GPU pool while giving paid tiers a meaningfully better experience under load?

As conversations get longer and usage scales up, how do you keep inference cost under control without making the assistant feel much slower or more forgetful?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Token streaming is the whole UX. The right shape is SSE from the web tier to the browser plus a pub/sub channel keyed by run id from the inference workers, so a stateless web pod can subscribe to a generation it did not start. The pattern article covers exactly this choice, pub/sub fanout versus a single connection-holding server, and why a stateless tier rules out the simple sticky path.

Read More
Managing Long Running Tasks

A generation runs up to 30 seconds, which is why requests go through a queue to a pull-based GPU worker pool with admission control and backpressure, and why cancellation has to be a first-class signal that stops the worker mid-stream. The long-running-tasks pattern is the playbook for queueing expensive work, bounding it, and stopping it cleanly.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

A turn is a small pipeline, load the chat's history, build the prompt (truncate or splice in the rolling summary), enqueue the generation, stream tokens, then persist the assistant message, plus an async job that refreshes the rolling summary. Keeping those stages isolated and recoverable so a failed generation does not corrupt the thread is what this pattern is about.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

PostgreSQL

System of record for users, chats, and messages, with cursor pagination on the messages table so a long thread does not load all at once. The full message log per chatId is also what the chat service reads to rebuild the prompt context on every follow-up turn. High availability over strict consistency is fine for this data, a slightly stale chat list is acceptable.

Read More
Redis

Carries the token stream: inference workers append token events to a stream or pub/sub channel keyed by run id, and whichever web pod holds the user's SSE connection subscribes and forwards them. It is also the natural home for the cost-aware per-user token budgets and tier rate-limit counters that the fairness logic debits on every submit.

Read More
API Gateway

The front door for authentication, request routing, edge rate limiting, and terminating the long-lived SSE connections so the stateless chat service only deals with conversation logic and dispatching generations. Centralizing those cross-cutting concerns is what keeps the chat tier thin and easy to scale out.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

WhatsApp

Same core data model and read path: users own conversation threads, threads own an ordered list of messages, you list threads and page through history, and new messages push to a live connection. ChatGPT swaps the second participant for an LLM and the push channel for a token stream, but the thread-and-message storage, ordering, and reconnect concerns are the same problem.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

The fairness deep dive is a rate limiter with a twist: the unit being limited is estimated GPU cost rather than request count, and there are tiered classes with different budgets and priorities. The mechanics, per-key token buckets, atomic debits, and a clear policy when the limit is hit, are exactly what a distributed rate limiter is built around.

Read
Practice
YouTube

Both put expensive, slow compute behind a job queue and a fleet of constrained workers, with the user waiting on the result of a single job. YouTube's transcoding pipeline and ChatGPT's GPU inference pool share the same shape: hand work off fast, pull when capacity exists, batch where you can, and shed load rather than let latency run away.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Returns the full response only after generation finishes

Waiting on the whole completion means a blank screen for up to 30 seconds and real timeout risk. Streaming token by token is a baseline expectation for this product. A strong candidate reaches for SSE early and treats time to first token as a design constraint, not an afterthought.

Stateful web server holding the inference connection, requiring sticky routing

If the web pod that called the worker has to be the one holding the browser connection, you have sticky routing browser to pod to worker and the design breaks the moment the web tier is stateless and connections are spread across pods. The fix is a pub/sub channel keyed by run id so any pod can subscribe to any generation. If a candidate keeps the connection state on the web server, push them on what happens during a deploy.

Web servers calling GPU workers directly with no queue, batching, or admission control

Direct dispatch has no place to do admission control, bursts pile onto busy workers and latency spikes, and the GPUs run one request at a time when continuous batching could keep them near saturated. The expected answer is a queue in front of a pull-based worker pool, batching on the inference side, and a backpressure policy. A candidate who skips this hasn't engaged with the actual bottleneck.

Treats a flat requests-per-minute cap as fairness

GPU cost tracks tokens, not requests, so a per-request cap lets one user with long prompts burn far more than their share, and it gives paid tiers no real priority. A strong answer measures usage in estimated token or compute cost, layers tier priority on top, and degrades free users first when the pool saturates. Push a candidate who proposes a single RPM number on the heavy-prompt case.

Sends the entire conversation into the model on every turn forever

Replaying full history makes each turn slower and more expensive as the chat grows and eventually overruns the context window. The expected progression is a rolling summary plus recent turns, ideally with prefix caching for the stable head of the prompt. Saying "just send all the messages every time" is fine for the first HLD pass but a red flag if it survives the cost deep dive.

Early March, 2026

OpenAI

Senior

They supplied initial requirements that changed throughout the interview. There was a focus on both the frontend and backend architectures.

Task: Design a basic AI chat application.
Initial Requirements:
- Users should be able to authenticate themselves.
- Users should be able to send messages and receive back a response from an AI.
- The AI response should stream in.
- If the user refreshes the page, they get a fresh start. No persistence of the conversation is needed.

Late February, 2026

OpenAI

Senior

Given a public streaming API that takes a list of messages and responds with AI response,

- Users should see streamed responses
- Users should be able to create, delete conversations
- Users should be able to name the conversation with AI
- Users should be able to see all past conversation history
